paper_title: Activity and Energy Expenditure in Older People Playing Active Video Games Lynne M. Taylor, MSc, Ralph Maddison, PhD, Leila A. Pfaefﬂi, MA, Jonathan C. Rawstorn, BSc, Nicholas Gant, PhD, Ngaire M. Kerse, PhD, MBChB
context: University research center; laboratory-based testing (playing area 4 x 4 m, 32-inch flat screen); Nintendo Wii and Xbox 360 Kinect platforms used.
duration: Each game played for 5 minutes; 5-minute seated rest interleaved between games; 20-minute familiarization; 10-minute supine rest for resting VO2.
treatment_raw: Participants played 9 active video games, each for 5 minutes, in random order. Two games (boxing and bowling) were played in both seated and standing positions.
control_raw: resting oxygen consumption per unit time (VˈO2) was measured while participants rested quietly, positioned supine for 10 minutes. Games were interleaved with 5 minutes of seated rest.
outcome_raw: energy expenditure (METs)
measurement_raw: indirect calorimetry; energy expenditure expressed in kilojoules per minute and metabolic equivalents (METs); VˈO2 measured via expired air gas analysis system
source_quote: Mean ± SD energy expenditure was signiﬁcantly greater for all game conditions compared with rest (all P≤.01) and ranged from 1.46±.41 METs to 2.97±1.16 METs.

paper_title: Activity and Energy Expenditure in Older People Playing Active Video Games Lynne M. Taylor, MSc, Ralph Maddison, PhD, Leila A. Pfaefﬂi, MA, Jonathan C. Rawstorn, BSc, Nicholas Gant, PhD, Ngaire M. Kerse, PhD, MBChB

control_raw: resting oxygen consumption per unit time (VˈO2) was measured while participants rested quietly, positioned supine for 10 minutes. Games were interleaved with 5 minutes of seated rest.
outcome_raw: difference in energy expenditure, activity counts, and perceived exertion between equivalent games played while standing and seated
outcome_canonical: no significant difference between seated and standing play (energy expenditure, activity counts, perceived exertion)
measurement_raw: indirect calorimetry; Actigraph accelerometers (hip and wrist) for activity counts; Ratings of Perceived Exertion (Borg scale) for perceived exertion
source_quote: There was no signiﬁcant difference in energy expenditure, activity counts, or perceived exertion between equivalent games played while standing and seated.

paper_title: Editor’s key points Painful medical procedures are often unavoidable in paediatric medical care, which can cause signiﬁcant pain, anxiety or distress, and can lead to negative long-term outcomes. Screen-based technologies can improve procedural
context: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of the effectiveness of interactive video games compared with standard care in children (0e18 yr) undergoing painful procedures.
duration: not_reported
treatment_raw: interactive video game interventions
control_raw: standard care or other non-interactive video game interventions
outcome_raw: children’s procedural pain
measurement_raw: standardised mean difference (SMD)
effect_size_raw: SMD¼e0.43; 95% conﬁdence interval (CI): e0.67 to e0.20
source_quote: interactive video games appear to reduce children’s procedural pain (standardised mean difference [SMD]¼e0.43; 95% conﬁdence interval [CI]: e0.67 to e0.20)

paper_title: Geoforum 121 (2021) 115–128 Available online 6 March 2021
population_raw: Chilean game developers and governmental agencies
context: Chilean video games sector, peripheral location; case study deploying semi-structured interviews with Chilean game developers and governmental agencies; discussion framed by dependency theory and cognitive capitalism.
duration: not_reported
outcome_raw: forms of dependency on the use of non-proprietary technological tools (game engines and software development kits – SDKs) and the protection of intellectual property (IP) in the centre (publishing schemes)

measurement_raw: semi-structured interviews; literature citations (Kirkpatrick, Dyer-Witheford, O'Donnell)
source_quote: Tools and software used for creating games are not proprietary software of small developers. Instead, these developers usually use software which is available for free, or on a licence basis, to create and edit games. The use of non-proprietary software has led to critiques from games scholars, specifically that the use of said tools (Game Engines and Software Development Kits for consoles) not only limits creativity (Kirkpatrick, 2013) but also imposes essential barriers for the dissemination of information among small actors.
notes: Blank effect evidence; excluded from graph weighting.
